title: Input Quality

content.question: What's one low-quality input you tolerate that should be cut this week?
sop: List your top 3 recurring inputs (leads, information sources, people, raw materials).
Rate the Input quality 1-10 and your Process quality 1-10 for each (see calculation, page 5)
Cut or upgrade the lowest-rated input first, a better process can't fully rescue a bad input.
Set a minimum standard/filter so low-quality inputs can't get in going forward.
Re-check output quality after 2 weeks to confirm the input change actually moved the result.
questions: What's one low-quality input you tolerate that should be cut this week?
Whose advice, content, or feedback have you been absorbing that's actually lowering your standards?
What raw material, leads, information, ingredients, are you accepting as 'good enough' without checking?

definitions.def: Garbage In, Garbage Out, the principle that flawed or low-quality inputs will always produce flawed outputs, no matter how good the process handling them is.

content.equation_example: Rate Input quality 1-10 and Process quality 1-10. Input 4 times Process 9 equals 36 out of 100. Higher means good material and good execution are compounding together. Lower means even a great process can't fully rescue poor inputs (or vice versa).
content.closing_statement: No process fixes what a bad input already broke.

caption:
Principle Nº 142: Input Quality

Outputs depend on inputs.

Ask yourself: What's one low-quality input you tolerate that should be cut this week?
